See /aː/. The short /a/ has a quality contrast as well as a length contrast. The pronunciation may be close to [ɐ]. The short /a/ occurs only in closed syllables, without final /ʔ/. Various script dialects will favor either U+AAB0 or U+AAB7.
Tai Dam has length contrast only between /aː/ and /a/.
fluctuates freely to [ɯ]
short /a/ + /p/ (alternative)
in open syllables only

See /aː/. The short /a/ has a quality contrast as well as a length contrast. The pronunciation may be close to [ɐ]. The short /a/ occurs only in closed syllables, without final /ʔ/. Various script dialects will favor either U+AAB0 or U+AAB7.

Linguists have differing views as to whether /ʂ/ or /ᶘ/ is the most appropriate phonetic representation of the Shaviyani character. Dr. Amalia Gnandesikan recommends /ʂ/, which follows current IPA guidelines for representing voiceless retroflex frica

Dr. Bruce Cain argues that /ᶘ/ is the more accurate phonemic representation for Shaviyani ‹ށ›. According to his data, Shaviyani is not pronounced as /ʂ/ in any context, and to render it so inaccurately suggests a similarity to o
In older orthography not distinguished from a bilabial voiced stop and thus written ‹ބ›.
In older orthography, not distinguished from a voiced dental stop and thus written ‹ދ›.
In older orthographies, not distinguished from a voiced velar stop, and thus written ‹ގ›.
In older orthography not distinguished from a voiced retroflex stop and thus written ‹ޑ›.
Occurs before a bilabial consonant (where it is pronounced [m]) or word finally (where it is pronounced [ŋ]).
Occurs before an palatal-alveolar consonant.

Linguists have differing views as to whether /ɾ/ or /ɻ/is the most appropriate phonetic representation of the Raa character.   Dr Amalia Gnandesikan, during her field research, was corrected when she pronounced Raa as an alveolar tap, and considers t
Only used for foreign words, to indicate English orthographic ‹r› in syllable codas (not pronounced in Dhivehi). In this use the‹ރ› will carry no vowel or sukun.

Linguists have differing views as to whether /ɾ/ or /ɻ/is the most appropriate phonetic representation of the Raa character. Dr. Bruce Cain maintains that Raa is never a retroflex approximant, and is best described as an alveolar tap.
Occurs before ‹ޖ› to indicate a geminate.

Occurs before ‹ވ› to indicate a geminate.
Occurs before ‹ޔ› to indicate a geminate.
Occurs before ‹ރ› to indicate a geminate; this phoneme may only be geminated in words of foreign origin.

Only in words of foreign origin. Occurs before ‹ޚ› to indicate a geminate.

Only in words of foreign origin. It occurs before ‹ޤ› to indicate a geminate.
Occurs before ‹ގ› to indicate a geminate.
Occurs in syllable codas, i.e. any time the /k/ is not immediately followed by a vowel. It is pronounced as [ʔ], [ŋ] or a gemination of the following consonant, depending on context.
When carrying a vowel rather than a sukun (null vowel) sign.
Prepausally pronounced [jʔ].
Only used for foreign words, to indicate English orthographic ‹g› after ‹n› (representing English /ŋ/). Silent in Dhivehi. In this use ‹ގ› will carry no vowel or sukun.
A rarely occurring phoneme.
A letter devised to transliterate Arabic ‹ث›. Occurs only in words of foreign origin.
A letter devised to transliterate Arabic ‹ح›. Occurs only in words of foreign origin and is often pronounced simply as /h/.
A letter devised to transliterate Arabic ‹خ›. Occurs only in words of foreign origin.
A letter devised to transliterate Arabic ‹ذ›. Occurs only in words of foreign origin.
A letter devised to transliterate Persian ‹ژ›. Occurs only in words of foreign origin.
A letter devised to transliterate Arabic ‹ش›. Occurs only in words of foreign origin.
A letter devised to transliterate Arabic ‹ص›. Occurring only in loanwords, it may be pronounced simply as /s/.
Only in words of foreign origin; it is a transliteration of the Arabic letter ‹ط›.
A letter devised to transliterate Arabic ‹ظ›. Occurs only in words of foreign origin and is generally pronounced /z/.
A letter devised to transliterate Arabic ‹ع›. Occurs only in words of foreign origin and is often left unpronounced.
A letter devised to transliterate Arabic ‹غ›. Occurs only in words of foreign origin.
A letter devised to transliterate the Arabic letter ‹ق›. It occurs only in words of foreign origin.
Only used for foreign words, a letter devised to transliterate Arabic ‹و› but rarely used, Thaana ‹ވ› being usually used instead.
In nonstandard dialects outside the capital.
In standard dialect, in which this vowel is long but has no short counterpart. In other dialects this vowel is /ai/.

The sixth vowel, often accented in dictionary headwords; the six vowels are written with only 5 symbols, so the two e’s are not distinguished; pronounced [ej] in many loans from Portuguese such as meja ‘table’
Typically from Arabic; tends to become /p/ in older borrowings (e.g. pikir ‘think’ is now more common than fikir)
Allophone: [ɪ]
Typically from Arabic; pronounced /k/ or /h/ by some speakers

Allophone: [r]
Typically from Arabic
Pronounced [f]; only used in loan words
Typically from Arabic; pronounced /j/ by many speakers; tends to become /j/ over time (e.g. jaman ‘era’ is now more common than zaman)
Often transcribed /ɯ/. The sound is similar to the Mandarin vowel transcribed [ɤ], hence the use of grapheme ‹e›, in parallel with Hanyu Pinyin.
Though some sources use a lateral fricative symbol, there is no audible lateral friction in Nuosu Yi.
Sometimes transcribed /ɛ/.
The Chinese tradition transcribes this sound using /ȵ/. Allophone /hny/ also uses /y/.
Syllable-final only.
Syllable-final only. There are two systematic spelling exceptions in this tone category: /u̱˥/ is spelled ‹ut› rather than *‹urt›, and /ɨ̱˥/ is spelled ‹yt› rather than *‹{lang:firs
Often transcribed /u /
Sometimes pronounced with heavy occlusion, e.g., syllable ‹mu› is commonly articulated as syllabic bilabial nasal.

Grapheme ‹w› represents syllable initial voiced velar fricative, or if not followed by a vowel, ‹w› romanizes the syllable iteration symbol ꀕ, which does NOT have a consistent phonetic value, but stands for r
Syllable-final only. Some sources transcribe 44 or ˦ for mid-high tone.
Often transcribed /ɿ̱/ apical vowel with 'tight throat'
Grapheme ‹y› is used for both a consonant and a vowel, as in syllable ‹yy› / ʑɨ / ‘water’.
Often transcribed /ɿ/ apical vowel.
There are 21 syllables that write no initial consonant.
Syllable-final only.
This grapheme is used for the second instance of a repeated syllable.

/au/ can be represented by ‹आउ›, ‹आु›, ‹ˌाˌु›, or ‹ˌाउ›.

/eu/ can be represented by ‹एउ›, ‹एु›, ‹ˌेˌु›, or ‹ˌेउ›.
/oi/ can be represented by ‹ओई›, ‹ओी›, ‹ˌोˌी›, or ‹ˌोई›.

intros.apd-Latn: Source: Hartell, Rhonda, ed., _Alphabets of Africa_. Dakar: UNESCO, 1993.(CR)(CR)See also [Systèmes alphabétiques des langues africaines](http://sumale.vjf.cnrs.fr/phono/PhonologieN.php).
intros.ar-Arab: This data is for Modern Standard Arabic (MSA).(CR)(CR)Sources:(CR)(CR)- [Wikipedia: Arabic, “Literary Arabic”](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic#Literary_Arabic)(CR)- [Wikipedia: Hamza](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hamza)(CR)- Daniels, Peter T. and William Bright, eds. 1996. _The World's Writing Systems_, p. 560(CR)- Mace, John. 2003. _Beginner's Arabic Script_.(CR)(CR)(CR)We did not include gemination over all consonants. It is likely that shadda (for gemination) can occur over other consonants. However, the use of shadda is rare in MSA and examples over other consonants proved difficult to find.(CR)(CR)This information is purely a start on documenting the phonemes for MSA. We welcome feedback.(CR)(CR)Contributor: Lorna Evans
intros.ast-Latn: The data shown below of graphemes and phonemes for Asturian is quite simplified, and is intended to represent his three dialectal areas (Western, Central and Eastern Asturian).(CR)(CR)(Source: Álvaro Arias, University of Oviedo)
intros.bcy-Latn: The data here only contained the Ɓacama (Ɓwaatye) phonemes.(CR)(CR)The data does not include the tones because there is no tone marking in the orthography; hence tone section is omitted here.(CR)(CR)Contributor: Kaduwe N. Ornan
intros.blt-Tavt: Tai Dam (ISO code blt) is a Tai language of Vietnam. It is primarily spoken in Sơn La province and surrounding areas of Vietnam and Laos. The Tai Viet script (ISO code Tavt) is the traditional script used by the Tai Dam and several closely related languages.(CR)(CR)There are a number of variations in the way the language is written. That shown here represents the writing of Baccam Don, from Thuan Chau in Sơn La Province.(CR)(CR)My primary sources are:(CR)(CR)- Baccam Don, Baccam Faluang, Baccam Hung and Dorothy Fippinger, _Tai Dam-English, English-Tai Dam Vocabulary Book_, 1989.(CR)- Fippinger, Jay and Dorothy Fippinger, “Black Tai Phonemes with Reference to White Tai,” _Anthropological Linguistics_, 12:83-97, March 1970.(CR)- Gedney, William J. “A Comparative Sketch of White, Black, and Red Tai.” _The Social Science Review_, Special Number (1964).(CR)(CR)(CR)Note that I use the “high” and “low” tone series labels as they are used by the language community, which are reflected in the Unicode character names, and which are the reverse of the way the labels are used in the Tai linguistic community.(CR)(CR)#### Vowels(CR)(CR)Like all Tai languages, Tai Dam vowels are written above, below, to the left, and to the right, of the initial consonant. A dotted circle is used here to show the position of the vowel relative to the initial consonant. But Tai Dam is visually encoded, so the left-side and right-side vowels are spacing characters, not diacritics, even though they are shown here with the dotted circle.(CR)(CR)#### Tone(CR)(CR)Tai Dam has six tones: four level tones and two glides. The tone numbering system most often used for the spoken language is that introduced by Gedney 1964, and Fippinger 1970:(CR)(CR)- Lower-mid level(CR)- High rising(CR)- Low: level or falling, terminated by a glottal catch(CR)- High level(CR)- Higher-mid level(CR)- Mid falling: abruptly falling from the level of tone 5, terminated by a glottal catch(CR)(CR)(CR)Only tones 2 and 5 occur on checked syllables (i.e. closed syllables ending with /p/, /t/, /k/, or /ʔ/).(CR)(CR)I have chosen to represent the level tones with the tone bars Extra High, High, Low, and Extra Low, respectively (omitting the Mid tone bar).(CR)(CR)In the written language, three different tone marking systems have been used for the Tai Viet in different times and places:(CR)(CR)- Historically, tones were not marked beyond the use of the high and low series consonants. In this system, tones are underdifferentiated. This system would be used in all documents before 1975, but is less common now.(CR)- Since 1975, one segment of the Tai Dam community has adopted the use of the Lao diacritic tone marks Mai Ek (U+AABF) and Mai Tho (U+AAC1).(CR)- Another segment of the Tai Dam community has created a pair of spacing tone marks, Mai Nueng (U+AAC0) and Mai Song (U+AAC2).(CR)(CR)(CR)Tone systems 2 and 3 are parallel to each other, with Mai Ek being equivalent to Mai Nueng, and Mai Tho equivalent to Mai Song.(CR)(CR)Tone system 1 can be derived from either of the other two by omitting the Mai Ek and Mai Tho, or Mai Nueng and Mai Song.(CR)(CR)I have chosen to describe systems 2 and 3 here.(CR)(CR)_Abbreviations:_ In the tone-related graphemes, -Low C- indicates the use of a Low Series Consonant for the intial consonant; -High C- indicates the use of a high series consonant in the initial position.(CR)(CR)Contributor: Jim Brase, SIL International
intros.de-Latn: This data applies to the standard High German dialect.
intros.dv-Thaa: The Thaana alphabet is unique to the Dhivehi language. The script contains two types of basic signs. The consonant signs are considered the actual letters of the alphabet, and they include a null consonant, ‹އ› (“alifu”). The consonant letters form the base line of writing, while the vowels signs—and the null vowel ‹ް› (“sukun”)--are diacritics written either above or below the consonant line, depending on the vowel. The writing of vowels is obligatory in Dhivehi.(CR)(CR)In addition to the standard Thaana alphabet, there is a set of extra letters, known as “thiki jehi Thaana” (“dotted Thaana”). These letters, which are formed from the basic Thaana letters by the addition of one or three dots above or below a Thaana letter, were designed to transcribe the Perso-Arabic script. The presence of such a dotted letter is a sure sign of a word of foreign origin. Many such words, if they are establised loans, have alternate nativized spellings without dotted letters. Note, however, that the ‹ޕ› (“paviyani”) has a dot but is part of the basic Thaana script, not the dotted series. Both the basic Thaana and the dotted Thaana letters are included in the table.(CR)(CR)The Thaana script accurately represents Dhivehi phonemes and morphophonology, but it has a number of systematic usages that are unusual, and which a phoneme-grapheme table obscures somewhat. One such usage is that of a gemination marker, ‹އް› (“alifu-sukun”), which geminates a following consonant (the alifu-sukun also writes a coda /k/, but if there is a following consonant, a /k/ in coda position will be pronounced as gemination of that consonant anyway). Because gemination is not considered a phoneme, the geminating effect of the ‹އް› is repeated many times in the accompanying table, with each geminatable consonant phoneme. Similarly, ‹ން›, writes any coda nasal, which will be pronounced as homorganic with a following consonant or as [ŋ] in the absence of a consonant. In the latter case, it may be underlyingly either /m/ or /n/. A ‹ނ› without a vowel sign or sukun (null-vowel sign) combines with a following consonant letter to form a digraph representing a prensalized stop.(CR)(CR)Vowels in Dhivehi may be either long or short. In general, the long vowels are doubled versions of the short vowels, but the doubled sign forms a single sign, not a digraph, in that it takes up the space of only a single vowel sign. The second part of a diphthong is written on a null consonant (“alifu”) sign.(CR)(CR)The first 9 letters of the Thaana alphabet (ie the list of consonant letters) are derived from the Arabic numerals 1 through 9, while the vowel signs for /a/, /i/, and /u/, as well as the null-vowel “sukun”, are derived from the optional vowel diacritics of the Arabic script. The names of many of the Thaana consonant letters are taken from the names of the corresponding letters of the Arabic script. Other consonant names end in -viyani.(CR)(CR)The examples listed in the table are taken from standard written Dhivehi, which is based on the northern dialect, particularly that of Malé. Certain systematic differences in other dialects are noted, however, and flagged in the notes.(CR)(CR)References for Thaana include the following: (1) DeSila, M.W.S (1969) The phonological efficiency of the Maldivian writing system. Anthropological Linguistics 11, 199-208. (2) Gair, J.W. & B.D. Cain (1996) Dhivehi Writing. In P.T. Daniels & W. Bright (eds.) The World's Writing Systems, 564-568, Oxford University Press. (3) Gnanadesikan, A.E. (2012) Maldivian Thaana, Japanese kana, and the representation of moras in writing. Writing Systems Research 4 (1-2), 91-102. (4) Gnanadesikan, A.E., R.W. McGowan, S.L. Rafalko, & K.S. Burk (2013) Thaana Basics: Getting Started with the Maldivian Alphabet. Technical Report, University of Maryland Center for Advanced Study of Language.(CR)(CR)Source: Amalia Gnandesikan
intros.en-Latn: The data shown below of graphemes and phonemes for English is quite simplified, and is intended to represent the Received Pronunciation of the UK and most North American dialects. A number of phonological issues, such as r-colored vowels (-er, -ar) and vowel length, are not included. Note also that the sounds indicated are phonemes, not actual phones, and as such do not reflect many subtleties of pronunciation. Also, only a small sample of words with unusual spellings have been included.(CR)(CR)A common phenomenon in English orthography is the vowel + consonant + silent e sequence (e.g., 'bake', 'home') where the presence of the silent e affects the pronunciation of the vowel. This is not represented in this data.(CR)(CR)One of the sources used in compiling this data was _The Writing Road to Reading_ by Romalda Bishop Spalding and Walter T. Spalding.(CR)(CR)To suggest improvements to the data, please contact the ScriptSource staff.
intros.es-Latn: The data shown below of graphemes and phonemes for Spanish is quite simplified, and is intended to represent a broad range of dialects. Specific differences between Iberian Spanish (as used in much of Spain) and Latin American Spanish are mentioned in notes. Note that the sounds indicated are phonemes, not actual phones, and as such do not reflect many subtleties of pronunciation.(CR)(CR)To suggest improvements to the data, please contact the ScriptSource staff.
intros.fub-Arab: This information comes from the Alphabet and Orthography Statement for Fulfulde [FUB] Ajamiya by Scott Clark, M.A.(CR)(CR)Source:[http://www.silcam.org/documents/AlphabetandOrthographyStatementforFulfuldeFUBAjamiyafortheinternet.pdf](CR)(CR)In the context of shadda + kasra, this language leaves the kasra below the consonant rather than following the Arabic practice of moving the kasra below the shadda(CR)(CR)Contributor: Lorna Evans
intros.fub-Latn: This information comes from the Alphabet and Orthography Statement for Fulfulde [FUB] Ajamiya by Scott Clark, M.A.(CR)(CR)Source:[http://www.silcam.org/documents/AlphabetandOrthographyStatementforFulfuldeFUBAjamiyafortheinternet.pdf](CR)(CR)Contributor: Lorna Evans
intros.ha-Latn: The modern Latin orthography for Hausa is called “boko”, made official during the 1930s by the British colonial administration.(CR)(CR)QUESTIONS:(CR)(CR)Does ts represent /ts/, /ts'/ or 's'/ (or all these allophones)?(CR)(CR)Sources:(CR)(CR)- Hartell, Rhonda, ed., _Alphabets of Africa_. Dakar: UNESCO, 1993.(CR)- _Handbook of the International Phonetic Association_. Cambridge: Cambridge UP, 1999.(CR)- Wikipedia, [Hausa language](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hausa_language).(CR)- [On-line Bargery Hausa-English Dictionary](http://maguzawa.dyndns.ws/)(CR)- Andy Warren-Rothlin, Matthew Harley, and Linda Horlings
intros.id-Latn: This is a straightforward coverage of the standard dialect of Indonesian as taught formally, used in speeches, etc.(CR)(CR)Other dialects tend to vary phonetically in fairly simple ways. (E.g. with a heavy Javanese accent, the affricates may be dental rather than alveolar, and voiced stops are extra long.)(CR)(CR)_Disclaimer:_ The inventory of diphthongs is tentative (off the cuff) and is not a serious claim in favor of diphthongs. Most adjacent vowels are probably just sequences.(CR)(CR)Contributed by: Jonathan Coombs
intros.ii-Yiii: Nuosu Yi (iii) is a Tibeto-Burman language variety spoken by about 3 million people in southern Sichuan Province, China. The writing system is syllabic. The character list shown here reflects the Standard Table of Yi Characters, as described in The Scheme for Yi Language Standardization (China State Council, 1980). This standardization uses symbols taken from the traditional Yi script as used in Liangshan, but uses the writing direction and punctuation of Modern Chinese, and reflects the phonology of the Shypnra dialect as spoken in Xide County. Graphemes are listed here in their Unicode order, which largely reflects their canonical order in dictionaries and other reference works. The grapheme ꀕ is used to indicate a repeated syllable, and therefore, in a canonically ordered list of words, may behave for ordering purposes like the glyph that proceeds it in that word.(CR)(CR)Texts written in the standardized syllabary are to be read from left to right, which is a reversal of the traditional practice in the Nuosu Yi area, where traditional Yi texts are to be read from right to left. Eight hundred twenty traditional characters or variants of traditional characters were chosen for the standardization, enough to render high, mid, and low tones unambiguous. To indicate the mid-high tone, the standardized system uses a diacritic, which whenever possible is placed over the corresponding mid tone character, and otherwise is placed over a low tone character.(CR)(CR)Besides the reversal of the reading direction, the two most notable changes made in the standardization process are the addition of Chinese-style punctuation, and the elimination of almost all variation in spelling. In contrast, most traditional Yi documents were written without any punctuation, and the author could choose among multiple characters or character variants for writing many of the syllables. Many of the less common traditional Yi characters were not chosen for use in the standardized syllabary, but most of the more widely used characters were chosen, even if their usage became more strictly defined. For example, ꀊ is widely used in the traditional system to write the syllable /ä̙/ in any of the four tones, and for the standardized syllabary it was chosen to represent specifically the mid tone version of the syllable: /ä̙˧/. To represent /ä̙/ with high or low tones, variant forms of the traditional character were chosen (ꀈ and ꀋ), and thus character variants became distinct characters when used in the standardized syllabary. Finally, a diacritic was added to ꀊ for the mid-high tone version of the syllable: ꀉ. The ancient practice of writing without any spaces between words was continued.(CR)(CR)The standardized Nuosu Yi syllabary is not the only Yi script. Not only is there the traditional Nuosu Yi script that formed the basis for this standardized syllabary (which itself has many local variants), but there are several other traditional Yi scripts used in Yunnan, Guizhou, and Guangxi, which differ not only in the language being written, but in the inventory of characters, the orientation of characters, the direction of writing, and the use of punctuation, as well as in whether or not there has been any attempt at standardizing the system.(CR)(CR)Examples are mostly drawn from dictionaries such as Bai (1990) and Ma, Walters, and Walters (2007).(CR)(CR)Contributors: Andrew Eatough and Dennis Walters
intros.ii-Latn: Nuosu Yi (iii) is a Tibeto-Burman language variety spoken by about 3 million people in southern Sichuan Province, China. The graphemes shown here reflect the Yiwen Guifan Fangʼan (The Scheme for Yi Language Standardization. China State Council, 1980). The syllabary and related graphemes are based on the Shypnra dialect as spoken in Xide County. This scheme writes forty three initial consonants, ten vowels, and four tone categories. The detailed phonemic analysis is described in Yiyu Jianzhi (Chen et.al., 1985) and others. Not all phonetic variants are listed here.(CR)(CR)The system was based on the Hanyu Pinyin romanization for Mandarin Chinese, in that Nuosu Yi sounds are when possible spelled as in Hanyu Pinyin, e.g., ‹b›, ‹p›, ‹m›, ‹f›, and new graphemes are introduced where Mandarin Chinese lacks a distinction found in Nuosu Yi, e.g. ‹bb›, ‹nb›, ‹hm›.(CR)(CR)This phonetic transcription has been adapted for cross-language comparison in Scriptsource, while seeking to respect other published descriptions. For instance, vowels ‹ie, a, uo, ur, yr› are here coded as RTR (retracted tongue root), as a representation of 紧喉 `tight throat’ given in Chinese sources such as Yiyu Jianzhi (Chen et.al. 1985. Beijing: Nationalities Publishing House. Pages 10-12 ) and Xiandai Yiyu (Ding et.al. 1991. Sichuan Nationalities Publishing House. Page 20).(CR)(CR)Contributors: Dennis Walters, et al.
intros.lbw-Latn: Tolaki (LBW) is a Celebic language (Austronesian › Malayo-Polynesian › Celebic) spoken by over 330,000 people in Southeast Sulawesi province, central Indonesia (see entry in Ethnologue). This data is for the Konawe and Mekongga dialacts, thus covering nearly 100% of the language community.(CR)(CR)The phonemic inventory is taken from _Tolaki Phonology_ by David Mead and Tambunan (unpublished typescript, 1993), and _Tolaki Phonology_ by Owen Edwards (draft, 2013). The orthography was developed and tested by Scott Youngman (1995-2013).(CR)(CR)Where Mead and Tambunan's phonemic analysis differs from Edwards, I have followed the former and made a note (applies to graphemes ‹b›, ‹d›, ‹e›, and ‹w›).(CR)(CR)Contributed by: Scott Youngman, June 12, 2013.
intros.nat-Latn: These data come from words collected in Flex (not yet published) by Ca̱hungwa̱rya̱ Language Project (CLP).(CR)(CR)Ca̱hungwa̱rya̱ [nat] has 5 different dialects, Makangara dialect is the reference dialect, chosen by the community leaders and representatives.(CR)(CR)Although Ca̱hungwa̱rya̱ is a tonal language, writing tone has yet to be tested. Probably grammatical tone should be indicated.
intros.ne-Deva: NB: “Status” refers not to how commonly that phoneme or that character appears in the language but to how common it is for that character to represent that phoneme or for that phoneme to be represented by that character.
intros.png-Latn: Grapheme inventory for Pangu [png], Niger State, Nigeria(CR)(CR)Contributor: James MacDonell
intros.ru-Cyrl: Note that Russian orthography is predominantly based on the concept of palatalization, which stems from the nature of consonants. Most consonants are paired as palatalized (also referred to as “soft”) and unpalatalized (“hard”). Russian palatalization is phonemic, with minimal pairs common, e.g. мат (/mat/ - ‘checkmate’) and мать (/matʲ/ - ‘mother’), and угол (/ugəl/ - ‘corner’) and уголь (/ugəlʲ/ - ‘coal’). Certain consonants do not have palatalized (or soft) counterparts, and are considered to be always “hard”, i.e. ж, /ʐ/; ш, /ʂ/; ц, /ʦ/; and others do not have unpalatalized counterparts, and are considered to be always “soft”, i.e. щ, /ʃ/ and ч, /ʧ/. Russian palatalized consonants also affect vowel orthography, creating a system of paired vowels as well (see note below). While it is inaccurate to describe vowels as being either “hard” or “soft”, the different written vowels are used to indicate that the preceding consonant sound is either “hard” (unpalatalized) or “soft” (palatalized). The vowels that are generally used following “hard” consonants are ‹a, э, о, у, ы›. Their counterparts, which are used following “soft” consonants, include ‹я, е, ё, ю, и›, respectively. The presence of a soft or hard consonant when it appears word finally is represented in the orthography by the presence or absence of the “soft sign” ‹ь›, while word medially the only indication of a consonant’s softness or hardness is usually the presence of the appropriate “soft” or “hard” vowel.(CR)(CR)Most Russian consonants can also be paired in terms of voicing, with the exception of ‹х, ц, ч, щ›, which have no voiced counterparts (except in rare instances with foreign words), and ‹л, м, н, р›, which have no voiceless counterparts. The presence or absence of voicing is important for pronunciation since Russian has both a regressive assimilation rule for the feature word-medially, as well as a word final devoicing rule. For example, /t/ (‹т›) is the voiceless counterpart to /d/ (‹д›), which in turn is the voiced counterpart to /t/ (‹т›). A /t/ (‹т›) will need to be voiced when immediately preceding a voiced consonant word-medially, resulting in a /d/ sound instead, while a /d/ will need to be devoiced either when immediately preceding a voiceless consonant or word-finally, resulting in a /t/ sound. The same is true for the “soft” forms of all “hard” consonants.(CR)(CR)**Note:** Consonant + vowel spelling conventions do not consistently reflect the distinction between palatal and non-palatal consonants. Therefore, some vowels that would usually signify a preceding palatalized (soft) consonant do appear following consonants that are always unpalatalized (hard). In such cases the vowel that is written does not reflect the actual pronunciation of the sound. This is particularly the case with both foreign loan words and also the case declensions of certain words (e.g. the prepositional case form of words like ). Of particular note is the fact that so-called “soft” vowels (‹е, ё, и, ю, я›) can follow those sibilant fricatives and affricates which do not have palatalized counterparts, i.e. xxx-Cyrl ж, /ʐ/; ш, /ʂ/; ц, /ʦ/. These consonants are never “softened”. In such cases, the following vowels are pronounced as their counterpart vowels which are used to indicate a preceding unpalatalized (hard) consonant. Examples include words like xxx-Cyrl цепь 'chain', /ʦɛpj/; xxx-Cyrl шесть 'six' /ʂɛstj/; жить 'to live', /ʐɨtj/; xxx-Cyrl Цю́рих 'zürich', /ʦuɾix/.(CR)(CR)For further information, I recommend the chapter on phonology found in _Russian: A Linguistic Introduction_, by Paul Cubberley.(CR)(CR)Since allophones are not represented in this data, the nuances regarding vowel reduction rules are not represented here.(CR)(CR)For the sake of clarity, stress is indicated with an acute accent in all multi-syllable words, even if an accent wouldn’t be included in practical orthography. Note that an acute accent is not used over the vowel ‹ё›, which is always stressed.(CR)(CR)Contributors: Marc Canner, Emily Roth

intros.wo-Latn: This information is based on the current orthography. It does not include obsolete characters.(CR)(CR)In Wolof, the letter q is equivalent to x after a nasal (xonx = xonq) or at a geminate xx (ñaxx -ñaq)(CR)(CR)Geminated consonants are documented because they are significant in the Arabic script orthography.(CR)(CR)Sources:(CR)(CR)- Hartell, Rhonda. 1993. _Alphabets of Africa._ Dakar, Senegal. UNESCO and Summer Institute of Linguistics.(CR)- Currah, Galen. [“Wolofal Orthography.”](http://www.paul-timothy.net/pages/wolof/wolofal/orthography/wolofal_orthography_currah_20-may-2011_a4.pdf) 20 May 2011. Web. Accessed 27 July 2015.(CR)- Sawyer, Richard. [“How to Make the Sounds in Wolof – Vowels.”](http://www.wolofresources.org/language/vowels.html) _Wolof of Senegal._ Web. Accessed 27 July 2015.(CR)(CR)(CR)Contributor: Lorna Evans
intros.wo-Arab: This information is based on the current orthography. It does not include obsolete characters.(CR)(CR)In Wolof, the letter q is equivalent to x after a nasal (xonx = xonq) or at a geminate xx (ñaxx -ñaq).(CR)(CR)Geminated consonants occur at the end of a word or before a suffix (shadda is used in both cases).(CR)(CR)Shadda is also used over the second consonant of a final, nasal consonant pair (as in sanc).(CR)(CR)Sukun only appears over an unreleased consonant.(CR)(CR)Sources:(CR)(CR)- Hartell, Rhonda. 1993. _Alphabets of Africa._ Dakar, Senegal. UNESCO and Summer Institute of Linguistics.(CR)- Currah, Galen. [“Wolofal Orthography.”](http://www.paul-timothy.net/pages/wolof/wolofal/orthography/wolofal_orthography_currah_20-may-2011_a4.pdf) 20 May 2011. Web. Accessed 27 July 2015.(CR)- Sawyer, Richard. [“How to Make the Sounds in Wolof – Vowels.”](http://www.wolofresources.org/language/vowels.html) _Wolof of Senegal._ Web. Accessed 27 July 2015.(CR)(CR)(CR)Contributor: Lorna Evans
